title: Parsing Error Messages
concept: Structuring error reports with context, type, and recommendations to help the model find solutions.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: when a tool fails, what should a well-behaved agent tool do instead of crashing the whole system?

options.text: Return a clear error message so the agent can decide how to adapt
Throw a system-level exception that stops everything
Silently do nothing and pretend it worked

exercises.explain: Graceful degradation means failing softly: hand the agent a clear error string instead of a hard crash, so it can choose a new plan.

cards.title: A cry for help that actually helps
cards.body: An error that just says 'Failed' tells the agent almost nothing. A good error message is like a helpful note that says exactly what broke and how to fix it.

cards.title: What is a structured error report?
cards.body: It is an error message organized into parts: the context (what was happening), the type (what kind of problem), and a recommendation (what to try next). This structure helps the AI find a solution faster.

cards.title: Like a doctor's report
cards.body: A doctor doesn't just say 'You're sick.' They note your symptoms, name the illness, and suggest a treatment. A structured error does the same for the agent.

cards.title: Weak vs strong error
cards.body: Weak: 'Error.' Strong: 'Context: fetching weather for city. Type: InvalidInput. Recommendation: check that the city name is spelled correctly.' The second one gives the agent a clear next move.

cards.title: Myth: more words = better error
cards.body: A long error dump isn't automatically helpful. What matters is clear structure: context, type, and a recommendation the agent can act on.

cards.title: Vague vs structured
cards.body: Structured errors turn confusion into a to-do list.

left.text: 'Something went wrong.' The agent has no idea what to fix.

right.text: Context + Type + Recommendation. The agent knows exactly what to try next.

exercises.prompt: Which three parts make a structured error report most useful to an AI agent?

options.text: The user's name, age, and location
A random error code with no meaning

exercises.explain: Context tells what was happening, type names the problem, and the recommendation suggests a fix. Together they point the agent toward a solution.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Adding a recommendation to an error message helps the agent decide what to try next.
exercises.explain: A recommendation is a hint for recovery. It saves the agent from guessing and speeds up finding a working solution.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing part of a structured error report.
exercises.sentence: A good error report names the type of problem, describes the context, and gives a recommendation for what to try next.
exercises.explain: The recommendation is the actionable hint that tells the agent how to recover from the error.

exercises.prompt: Which error message was written by an AI to be clear and structured for an agent?

options.text: Context: saving file to disk. Type: PermissionDenied. Recommendation: try a different folder or request write access.
ugh it broke again lol try later maybe

exercises.explain: The AI-written message is organized into context, type, and a clear recommendation, exactly what an agent needs to recover.

exercises.prompt: An agent calls a tool to book a flight and gets back: 'Error.' Nothing else. Using what you learned about graceful degradation AND structured error reports, what is the BEST way to rewrite this so the agent can recover?

options.text: 'Context: booking flight for June 5. Type: NoSeatsAvailable. Recommendation: try a nearby date or a different airline.'
Throw a system exception so the whole agent crashes and a human must restart it
Return a huge unstructured dump of every internal variable with no summary

exercises.explain: The best answer combines both ideas: it degrades gracefully (a clear string instead of a crash) AND is structured with context, type, and a recommendation. That gives the agent everything it needs to adapt and try a new plan on its own.
